retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Technique or Treatment -- Advantages
retrieved_docs.content: The main advantage of air-powder polishers is their ability to efficiently remove biofilm without harming the periodontal soft tissues or hard tissue structures. This method is faster and provides more patient comfort. It saves time, and hence, it can prevent patient and operator fatigue. It can be used on orthodontically bracketed teeth as it does not physically damage or disturb the bands, wires, or bonding cement. It reduces dentinal sensitivity by blocking the tubular opening with bicarbonate crystals. It reaches inaccessible areas that cannot be reached by rotary devices. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Enamel has an organized and complex structure, fundamental to its mechanical performance. The hydroxyapatite crystals align to form enamel rods (previously known as prisms) enclosed in a sheath of the organic matrix. [4] The rods follow an undulating path from the enamel-dentine junction (EDJ) towards the outer occlusal surface, where their arrangement is more parallel. [5] The inner enamel layer's decussating pattern creates Hunter-Schreger bands (viewable under reflected light), accounting for its resistance to crack formation. Within the oral cavity, dental enamel can repeatedly withstand masticatory forces of up to 770 newtons, owing to its resilience. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Technique or Treatment -- Air-Powder Polishers
retrieved_docs.content: The air powder polisher can also be used with an ultrasonic scaler, directly with the air/water connector, or separately. It works with foot control. The nozzle of the handpiece has to be held 3 to 4 mm from the tooth as it propels the slurry of water and sodium bicarbonate on the tooth. The motion used in air powder polishers is a paintbrush motion at 60 degrees for anterior teeth, an 80-degree angle for posterior teeth, and 90 degrees on the occlusal surfaces. It should be directed at the middle thirds of the tooth in a circular motion with an air pressure of 40 to 100 psi and inlet water pressure of 20 to 60 psi. [7] Adjusting the water flow and the distance between the instrument and the tooth helps to adjust abrasive forces. They are generally safe for exogenous stain removal except for exposed dentin or cementum regions, which can be damaged because of the abrasives in the air-powder polishers. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Dentine constitutes the main bulk of the tooth. Its global distribution can be broadly separated into three categories: a mineral phase, an organic matrix, and water, forming 70%, 20%, and 10%, respectively, of its weight. [6] The most distinctive dentine feature is its honeycomb-like network of tubules, which extend outwards from the pulp towards the EDJ. At the dental pulp and dentine interface, the density and diameter of the tubules are highest, and this decreases with proximity to the EDJ. The dentinal tubules allow for the pulp's communication with the mineralized tissues and permit the transduction of physical signals to sensory responses. Their structure is used for bonding adhesive materials in some restorative dental treatment procedures. [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Porosity and Material-Dentine Interface
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine demonstrated lower porosity when used in a continuously moist environment, although it might present challenges in dry conditions. Research indicated that Biodentine adapts well to dentine due to micromechanical adhesion. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel-Dentin Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth fragment is available, reattachment should be considered. The fragment must be soaked in water or saline solution for 20 minutes before the procedure to restore tissue hydration. Other treatment options include direct composite restoration, composite restoration with a waxup, and porcelain and resin-based indirect restorations.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Dentin is less hard than enamel but still harder than bone and is composed of weight 70% mineralized material, 20% organic material, and 10% water. The crystals mainly consist of calcium hydroxyapatite Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2 and small percentages of carbonate and fluoride. Dentin protects the pulp and has great tensile strength.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Enamel is a non-renewable tissue, and enamel preservation is crucial for the dental professional. The physical properties and histology must be considered when treating and counseling patients, and appropriate knowledge of enamel sealants, polishing, and other products is pivotal.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Setting Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The retarder can slow the setting reaction, giving rise to dental alginates with different working times. Type I is a fast set that hardens within 1 to 2 minutes, and type II is a standard set that hardens within 2 to 5 minutes. [3] The setting reaction time of dental alginates also depends on the water temperature used for the mixture. Warmer water speeds up the setting reaction, and colder water slows the reaction down. [3] The water-to-powder ratio also affects the setting reaction; the hardening reaction is faster with more powder. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Equipment -- Polishing Devices
retrieved_docs.content: Manual: Handheld devices Engine-driven: Require handpieces

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Preparation and Restoration of the Tooth
retrieved_docs.content: Cleaning: A pumice slurry should be applied to the tooth surface using a prophy cup. Conditioning of tooth: after rinsing and drying the tooth, 10% polyacrylic acid is applied for 10 seconds to increase the surface energy and wettability of the tooth, which improves chemical bonding. [4] Placement of restoration: the mixed GIC is carried to the cavity with the help of a cement carrier and adapted using a condenser. Cement sticking to the spatula should be avoided as it is difficult to remove. Protection of the cement: the setting cement should be covered by a matrix band during the initial setting and by varnish, cocoa butter, or vaseline after the initial set because GIC is moisture sensitive during the first 24 hours of setting. Finishing and polishing: initial finishing includes the removal of gross excess with a sharp hand instrument. Final finishing is done after 24 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Technique or Treatment -- Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Air polishers are generally used on supragingival surfaces. Still, glycine powder air polishers are used for removing sub-gingival biofilm, resulting in less erosion of the soft tissues and an 80% reduction in abrasion of the root surface as compared to hand instrumentation or sodium bicarbonate air polishing. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Manipulation
retrieved_docs.content: The first increment is added to the liquid and mixed using a folding motion for 15 seconds within a limited area on the mixing pad (see Image . Folding Glass Ionomer Cement). The second increment is added to adjust the consistency. The resultant mixture should have a glossy surface (see Image . Freshly Mixed Glass Ionomer Cement). Such a surface indicates that an adequate number of carboxylic ions are available, crucial for forming chemical bonds with the tooth surface. Mixtures with an opaque surface should be discarded (see Image . Poor Mix Adhesion).

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel-Dentin Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: Exposed dentinal tubules must be sealed with a bonding agent and restored with composite resin or glass ionomer. If the exposed dentin is close to the pulp, indicated by a slight pink hue without bleeding, calcium hydroxide may be used as a lining material before placing a glass ionomer cement restoration.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Setting Reaction and Interaction with Dentin
retrieved_docs.content: The setting reaction of Biodentine involves a hydration process, during which the powder and liquid components are mixed using an amalgamator. This trituration process results in a creamy paste. The reaction of the powder with the liquid forms a silicate hydrate gel and calcium hydroxide (CH). [1] Calcium hydroxide dissociates into hydroxyl (OH − ) and calcium ions (Ca 2+ ), increasing the pH and Ca 2+ concentrations. [1] This release of Ca 2+ contributes to the material's bioactivity and apatite-forming properties and stimulates dental pulp cell differentiation, ultimately promoting dentin bridge formation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Abrasive agents are used for polishing to make teeth lustrous and give smooth and shiny surfaces. They are included in dentifrices and polishing pastes, with the difference that the particles are bigger in the latter.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. Across these preparations, sodium monofluorophosphate provides fluoride ions that inhibit demineralization and promote remineralization of tooth enamel, thereby reducing the incidence of dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 907
retrieved_docs.content: process without contributing therapeutic activity. Calcium carbonate is also present as an inactive ingredient in the oral rinse “sodium fluoride 0.5 MG/ML Mouthwash”, where it likely serves as a buffering agent. The supplement “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron” is described as having a narrower relationship to calcium carbonate, indicating that calcium carbonate is a key, though not exclusive, component of its formulation. Finally, the material “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is linked to calcium carbonate through a “has child” relationship, reflecting the natural derivation of nacre from calcium carbonate crystals. Collectively, these relationships illustrate calcium carbonate’s versatile role in human and veterinary medicines—as a therapeutic calcium source, a pH‑modulating agent, a filler or excipient, and even as a structural component of natural biomaterials—underscoring its importance across diverse therapeutic contexts and product types.

Together, these relationships illustrate that ammonium chloride functions both as a key active electrolyte component in multi‑herbal oral liquid preparations and as a member of a broader electrolyte/mineral replacement group that includes related compounds such as chromic chloride, while also co‑existing with inactive constituents like selenium sulfide in certain formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 40
retrieved_docs.content: therapeutic magnesium source and as a multifunctional excipient, while calcium carbonate serves as a key antacid, nutrient, and excipient across antacid, dental, nutritional, enzymatic and antibiotic products. Recognising these relationships aids clinicians, formulators and patients in understanding ingredient composition, potential drug‑nutrient interactions, and the rationale behind excipient selection in patient‑focused medication design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 839

A hierarchical relationship is also recorded in which “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is a child of calcium carbonate, reflecting the natural derivation of mother‑of‑pearl from calcium‑carbonate‑based shells. Collectively, the community knowledge portrays calcium carbonate as a versatile agent that can act as a calcium source, antacid, buffering or pH‑adjusting component, filler, binder, and texturizing/remineralizing additive across human nutraceuticals, veterinary antibiotics, dental formulations, and digestive‑enzyme supplements, while sodium monofluorophosphate is consistently the active fluoride agent in topical dental products for caries prevention.

Overall, the knowledge network connects caries‑preventive agents, fluoride‑based supplements, and various oral care preparations through shared classifications (stomatological preparations), chemical constituents (tin fluoride, sodium fluoride, sodium monofluorophosphate), and product‑level active‑ingredient relationships, underscoring the central role of fluoride compounds in dental prophylaxis and therapeutic oral formulations.

Sodium monofluorophosphate consistently serves as the active fluoride agent in dental pastes, with specific concentrations ranging from 0.0076 mg/mg to 2.43 mg/g, underscoring the importance of contextual interpretation of ingredient classifications in pharmaceutical and oral‑health products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: the added iron. In oral hygiene, the mouthwash “sodium fluoride 0.5 MG/ML Mouthwash” includes calcium carbonate as an inactive ingredient, where it may act as a buffering agent or aid in the formulation’s stability. Finally, the term “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is linked to calcium carbonate through a “has child” relationship, reflecting that calcium carbonate is a constituent material of the natural nacreous substance. Collectively, these relationships illustrate calcium carbonate’s versatile role as both an inactive excipient and an active therapeutic component, its presence in diverse dosage forms (granules, powders, oral solutions, and supplements), and its relevance to product stability, patient compliance, and clinical efficacy across veterinary and human medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: with Calcium and Extra Iron. The mechanism of action attributed to calcium carbonate involves participation in electrolyte activity, modulation of calcium ion balance, and contribution to acid‑base homeostasis. Clinically it functions as an antacid for gastro‑esophageal reflux disease (GERD), reflux esophagitis, dyspepsia and diarrhea, providing symptomatic relief by neutralizing gastric acidity. Calcium carbonate is also noted as a downstream component of natural mineralized structures such as pearls. Sodium monofluorophosphate, the active fluoridated compound in the dental pastes, is present at concentrations from 0.22 µg/100 µg to 2.43 mg/g and is formulated together with calcium carbonate (0.0076–0.0088 mg/mg) to prevent dental caries. From a gastrointestinal classification perspective, diverticulosis and diverticulitis are grouped within the “unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder” SMQ (الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي SMQ), a category that also includes
